speaker: ALEX
text: For a learner, the useful signals are concrete. main is always deployable. Nothing goes into main directly - every change goes through a PR and review. speaker: ALEX
text: Start here: Developers branch feature/my-feature off develop and work there. Then: Completed features merge back into develop via pull request. That small check between steps is what makes the workflow reliable.

text: If someone is taking notes, this is the short list. The six steps (branch, commit, PR, review, checks, merge) map to six distinct pages on GitHub; you can verify your stage by pressing 1 to hear the page title on each. When you open a PR, press D to the "Pull request navigation tabs" landmark; the Conversation tab confirms your PR is open and shows the linked issue. After merge, press G I to jump to the Issues tab and verify the linked issue closed automatically (it now shows a purple "Closed" badge). Each PR in the Pull Requests list has a colored icon: green circle for open, purple merged icon for merged, red circle for closed. The "Compare & pull request" yellow banner appears at the top of the repo after pushing a branch; it is full-width and prominent at any zoom level. Before merging, the status checks area below the PR description shows green checkmarks (passed) or red X marks (failed); zoom in to read individual check names.

text: On the ground, that means a few things. In the GitHub web interface, press D to cycle landmarks until you reach the branch region, then press B to find the "Sync fork" button. text: A few details make that real. In VS Code's Source Control panel (Ctrl+Shift+G), the commit message input is the first field; type your message there and press Ctrl+Enter to commit. On the Commits tab of a PR, press 3 to jump between date-group headings, then I to navigate individual commits; each commit announces its message and author. Good commit messages start with a verb in imperative mood ("Add," "Fix," "Remove"); your screen reader will read these as the first word when navigating commit lists. In the commit history view, only the first line (subject) of each commit message is visible by default; click "." to expand the full body. Keep the subject line under 50 characters so it does not truncate in GitHub's commit list view at any zoom level. VS Code shows a vertical ruler in the commit message field at 72 characters; lines longer than this may wrap awkwardly in terminal and email displays.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on The Nature of Open Source Communication. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: The reason this matters is simple: open source collaboration happens primarily in writing, asynchronously, in public. The listener should be able to check this: Understanding these three characteristics shapes everything about how we communicate.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the teaching thread moving. In writing has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the part to remember. There is no tone of voice, body language, or immediate clarification. A message that sounds terse in your head may read as hostile to the reader. Sarcasm and irony are nearly impossible to convey safely - avoid them. Solution: Be explicit. "I think this might cause a problem because." is clearer than "This is problematic.".

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's pause on Asynchronously. What should a learner take away from it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Asynchronously has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the part to remember. Comments are not instant messages - the reader may see your post hours or days later. You may be in a rush; they are not receiving urgency from your message. Comments exist without the context of what you were thinking when you wrote them. Solution: Provide all necessary context in every message. Do not assume continuity.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the teaching thread moving. In public has three jobs: name the idea, give the learner a move, and show what counts as evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the part to remember. Everything you write is visible to everyone, forever, and may be indexed and shared. Future contributors, employers, and the broader community will read your words. A dismissive reply to a beginner casts a shadow on the entire project. Solution: Write as if your most supportive and most critical reader are both watching.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What do you want them to do when the plan breaks?

speaker: ALEX
text: The teaching point here is not the label; it is the move. Whether commenting on an issue, reviewing a PR, or responding to a question, effective feedback has a structure.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the teaching thread moving. This part earns its place because before identifying problems, name what is good. That gives the learner a foothold: this is not flattery - it is accuracy. That is the difference between following directions and owning the workflow.

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the teaching thread moving. Anchor this part on 5. Signal the weight of the concern. Help contributors understand what is a blocker versus a preference. The durable skill is not memorizing one screen. It is knowing how to find your footing when the screen changes.

speaker: ALEX
text: Here is the part to remember. nit: - minor, optional suggestion ("nit: there's a trailing space here"). No qualifier - normal concern, should be addressed. "This is a blocker because." - must be fixed before merge. "Just a thought, not a blocker." - feedback but no requirement.
